title: Dyeing with eucalyptus
subtitle: How to use fresh or dried eucalyptus leaves to dye wool a range of tan, ochre, and rust tones with an alum mordant
excerpt: Eucalyptus leaves produce warm tan-to-ochre tones on alum-mordanted wool, shifting toward deeper rust or khaki when iron is added as a modifier. Fresh leaves give slightly more colour than dried, but both work well. The dye bath has good exhaustion, making it suitable for successive dyeing of lighter values.
sourceNotes: Eucalyptus dyeing is documented in Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour' and in Sasha Duerr's work. Colour ranges and mordant quantities are consistent across UK and US natural dye guild instruction. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Potassium aluminium sulphate used to fix natural dye to protein fibres. Applied at 10 to 15 percent of the dry weight of fibre (WOF), dissolved in warm water and simmered with the fibre before dyeing. Alum is the safest and most widely used mordant for home natural dyeing.

Ferrous sulphate added after dyeing to deepen and shift a colour. Iron saddens (darkens) and cools most natural dye colours: a warm tan from eucalyptus becomes a khaki or olive after a short iron modifier bath. Use sparingly — too much iron weakens protein fibres over time.

The dry weight of the fibre being dyed, abbreviated WOF, used as the basis for calculating mordant and dye quantities. A recipe calling for 15 percent mordant WOF means 15 g of mordant per 100 g of dry fibre. Always weigh fibre dry, before soaking.

content.text: Eucalyptus leaves contain tannins and phenolic compounds that produce good dye uptake on

wool. The colour range depends on the eucalyptus species: Eucalyptus cinerea (silver dollar) and E. globulus (blue gum) are commonly available in the UK and both give warm tans and ochres. An optional
bath after dyeing shifts the colour toward khaki or dark olive. Calculate mordant and dye quantities against the
. Eucalyptus can be grown in sheltered UK gardens and in containers. The Garden category has guidance on growing eucalyptus for both foliage and dyeing.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Alum is safe at dye quantities. Use separate vessels from food. Keep iron modifier away from skin and dispose of exhaust responsibly.

items.name: Large stainless steel dye pot, at least 6 litres

content.text: Dissolve 15 g of alum in 3 litres of warm water in the dye pot. Add the pre-wetted wool and heat gradually to 80°C over 30 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, stirring gently. Remove the wool, squeeze gently, and set aside. Do not rinse between the mordant bath and the dye bath: the alum must remain in the fibre. The mordant bath can be cooled and reused for a second batch.

Place the eucalyptus leaves in 3 litres of cold water in the dye pot and heat slowly to 80°C. Simmer for 45 to 60 minutes until the liquid is a deep amber. Strain out the leaves and return the liquid to the pot. Add the mordanted wool to the warm dye bath (not hot: entering very hot liquid can shock the fibre). Heat the dye bath back to 75 to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes, stirring gently.

For a khaki or olive result: dissolve 2 g of ferrous sulphate in a small amount of hot water and stir into a separate pot of warm water. Transfer the dyed wool directly from the dye bath into the iron modifier bath for 10 to 15 minutes at 60 to 70°C. Watch closely: iron works quickly and excess iron degrades wool. Rinse thoroughly after the iron bath.

Rinse the dyed wool in progressively cooler water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing and hang to dry away from direct sunlight. Eucalyptus-dyed wool has good washfastness with an alum mordant and can be washed at cool temperatures.

items.problem: The colour is very pale despite a good dye bath
items.cause: Mordanting was skipped or insufficient, or the fibre was not fully saturated before mordanting
items.fix: Eucalyptus requires a mordant to achieve full colour depth on wool. Re-mordant the pale wool with a fresh alum bath and then re-dye in a new dye bath. The result will be deeper on the second pass.

items.problem: The iron modifier produced a grey-green rather than the khaki expected
items.cause: Excess ferrous sulphate was used, overshooting the desired shift
items.fix: Use no more than 2 g of ferrous sulphate per 100 g of fibre and keep the modifier bath under 15 minutes. The colour shift from iron is rapid; check the fibre every 5 minutes and remove as soon as the desired tone is reached.
